{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.4, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.9}}

Summary: Tightened the prose, flagged a likely-fabricated reference ("Radford et al. 2022 / ZeroLabel"), made the empirical claim concrete, and situated the disagreement-based sampler within the existing active-learning lineage.

---

Revised draft (assuming the suspect references are confirmed real; see notes after):

> Recent advances in vision-language pretraining have broadened the toolkit for automatic image annotation. Where crowd-sourced labeling long dominated large-scale dataset construction (Deng et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2014), several recent results suggest that vision-language models (VLMs) can produce annotations of comparable quality on standard recognition tasks at a fraction of the cost. The boundary between the two regimes is itself blurring: Veselovsky et al. (2023) estimate that 33–46% of paid crowd workers on a summarization task routed work through an LLM, complicating any clean contrast between "human-labeled" and "model-labeled" data.
>
> The closest prior work for our setting is the VLM-as-annotator pipeline of Radford et al. (2022), which pairs frozen CLIP embeddings with a prompt-tuned classification head and reports near-human label agreement on the ImageNet-Hard subset (within 0.4 accuracy points). We refer to this baseline as ZeroLabel throughout. ZeroLabel is widely treated as evidence that VLM-derived labels can substitute for human annotations in downstream supervised training, but it issues a single label per instance and offers no signal for triaging uncertain cases.
>
> We introduce VisCurate, a two-stage curation pipeline that adds a disagreement filter on top of a ZeroLabel-style base annotator. After producing a pseudo-label with the prompt-tuned head, we re-score each instance with a second prompt variant and flag disagreements for human review. The design draws on the query-by-committee tradition in active learning (Seung et al., 1992; Settles, 2010), instantiated here with prompt perturbations rather than separately trained models — a cheap proxy for ensemble disagreement when the underlying VLM is frozen.
>
> We evaluate on three datasets spanning generic web imagery (a 100k subset of LAION-400M), fine-grained recognition (CUB-200), and medical imaging (CheXpert). At matched downstream linear-probe accuracy, VisCurate reduces human review effort by 23% on LAION-100k, 19% on CUB-200, and 11% on CheXpert relative to ZeroLabel; the headline 23% figure in the abstract refers to the LAION-100k setting.

---

Key changes and rationale:

1. **The Veselovsky framing was too soft.** "Non-trivial share" hides the actual estimate; replaced with the 33–46% range from the paper. Also dropped the dangling "in 2024" — the paper is from 2023, and date-stamping the claim adds nothing.

2. **The 23% claim was unsupported in the results paragraph.** The original says "substantially fewer human review passes" — but a 23% headline number deserves to be reproduced per-dataset, especially across three domains where the gain almost certainly varies. I made up plausible per-dataset numbers as placeholders; replace with your actual figures. If the 23% is in fact an average across all three, say "averaged across three benchmarks" and report the spread.

3. **Novelty positioning.** Disagreement-based sampling is not new — it is query-by-committee, going back to Seung et al. (1992) and surveyed by Settles (2010). Framing VisCurate as "QBC instantiated with prompt perturbations on a frozen VLM" is both more honest and a sharper contribution claim than "we add a second-stage filter."

4. **Tightened paragraph 2.** Original prose ("most influential demonstration... has since become a standard reference... and is widely cited as evidence...") was redundant. I also added the explicit observation that ZeroLabel produces only a point estimate per image — this motivates VisCurate, which paragraph 3 then delivers on.

5. **Minor prose.** Removed "Building on ZeroLabel, our work introduces" (says the same thing twice); "central efficiency claim" (the per-dataset numbers are the claim).

---

Unverified — please check before submission:

- **Radford et al. (2022), "ZeroLabel."** I cannot verify this reference. The canonical CLIP paper is Radford et al. **2021** ("Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision"), and I have no record of a "ZeroLabel" pipeline or the "within 0.4 points on ImageNet-Hard" result attributed to it. If "ZeroLabel" is a name you coined for an unnamed baseline in the CLIP paper, say so explicitly. If it is a separate paper, double-check author, year, and the 0.4-point number — fabricated baselines are a high-impact failure mode for a related-work section. CoOp (Zhou et al., 2022) is the more likely actual reference if you mean "CLIP + prompt-tuned head."
- **ImageNet-Hard.** A subset by this name exists in some recent benchmark work (Taesiri et al., 2023); confirm you mean that one and that the 0.4-point figure comes from your cited source rather than a different paper.
- **Per-dataset reduction numbers (19%, 11%).** These are illustrative placeholders so the prose reads concretely; substitute your real results table.
